talk_title: From fusion biology to functional genomics and therapeutic strategies in pediatric cancers

speaker.name: Dr. Kimberly Stegmaier
speaker.affiliation: Department of Pediatric Oncology, Dana-Farber Cancer Institute

biomarkers.name: STAG2 loss
biomarkers.finding: STAG2 loss associated with higher incidence of metastatic disease and poorer survival in Ewing sarcoma

biomarkers.name: RCST1-ABL2 fusion
biomarkers.finding: Patient with B-ALL harboring RCST1-ABL2 fusion achieved complete remission when imatinib was added to chemotherapy

models.name: LEAP (Leukemia Precision Therapy Consortium)
models.genotype_or_key_features: genomics‑matched targeted therapy for pediatric leukemia

MOA_summary: Stegmaier's work integrates large‑scale functional genomics (CRISPR and chemical screens) with fusion biology to identify pediatric cancer dependencies such as TRIM8, ETV6, and UGT3A2, revealing a Goldilocks dosage requirement for EWS‑FLI1. She leverages degrader technologies (PROTACs, molecular glues) and bifunctional molecules to selectively degrade or rewire the fusion protein, demonstrating that loss of TRIM8 elevates EWS‑FLI1 levels and that targeted degradation of the fusion can induce apoptosis in Ewing sarcoma models.
key_findings: EWS‑FLI1 dosage is a Goldilocks dependency: both over‑expression and complete repression are deleterious to Ewing sarcoma cells.
TRIM8 is a selective Ewing sarcoma dependency; its knockout increases EWS‑FLI1 levels and is lethal in vitro and in vivo.
ETV6 competes with EWS‑FLI1 on chromatin; CRISPR or dTAG‑mediated loss of ETV6 leads to excess EWS‑FLI1 activity and cell death.
STAG2 loss predicts metastatic disease and poor prognosis in Ewing sarcoma, validated in isogenic knockout models.
Chemical PRISM screening identified compound R112 as uniquely active in Ewing sarcoma; activity is rescued by UGT3A2 knockout, implicating UDP‑glucose metabolism as a novel vulnerability.
A genomics‑guided case of a pediatric B‑ALL patient with RCST1‑ABL2 fusion achieved durable complete remission when imatinib was added to standard therapy.
Development of high‑throughput GFP‑mCherry reporter assays enabled screening of >100,000 molecules, yielding several candidates that induce proteasome‑dependent degradation of the EWS‑FLI1 fusion.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Goldilocks phenomenon indicates that precise modulation of fusion protein levels is required; both hyper‑activation and complete loss are cytotoxic, complicating therapeutic windows.
STAG2 loss creates a selective vulnerability but also promotes metastatic behavior, suggesting potential resistance via lineage plasticity.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target the RCST1‑ABL2 kinase fusion identified by genomics in relapsed B‑ALL
combination_strategies.phenotype: Complete remission in a patient refractory to chemotherapy and CAR‑T

combination_strategies.rationale: Enhance efficacy of targeted agents (e.g., imatinib) in genomics‑matched leukemia
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved remission rates in LEAP cohort (specific numbers not disclosed)

combination_strategies.rationale: Rescue viability loss caused by degrader candidates, confirming proteasome‑dependent mechanism
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reversal of GFP‑fusion loss in reporter assays

open_questions: What are the precise molecular mechanisms by which UGT3A2 mediates sensitivity to R112 in Ewing sarcoma?
Can bifunctional degraders be developed that directly engage EWS‑FLI1 without requiring a high‑affinity ligand?
How can the Goldilocks dosage requirement be translated into a therapeutic index that avoids toxicity in normal tissues?
